fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: Small caps have delivered high growth over long periods. This fund has strong 3-year returns and manageable yearly fee. Since you’re 33 and investing for 10–20 years, it can help you aim for the 5Cr target.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap funds balance growth and risk. This one has been consistent with strong long-term returns. Fits your aggressive style and long-term goals.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Flexi cap funds pick stocks across all sizes. This fund is big, trusted, and has steady returns. It reduces risk by not being stuck in just one type of stock.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: ELSS gives tax savings under Section 80C. You’re in the 30% tax slab, so this saves ₹9,360 per year. It also behaves like an equity fund after 3 years. Good to start with now for long-term tax efficiency.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital Equity Savings Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Equity savings funds are taxed like stocks (favourable at 20%/30% slab) but are less volatile. We use lumpsum here to park your existing ₹25L for safer growth while you build the SIP plan.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin by Zerodha.
Step 2: Complete KYC if not already done (uses Aadhaar and PAN).
Step 3: Link your bank account.
Step 4: Start your monthly SIPs: ₹1.05L in Bandhan Small Cap, ₹75K in Nippon Mid Cap, ₹45K in HDFC Flexi Cap, ₹30K in WhiteOak ELSS.
Step 5: Invest ₹25L as lumpsum in WhiteOak Equity Savings Fund.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs are running smoothly. See if any fund is behind its peers.
After 1 year: Review annual returns. Compare your portfolio to similar funds.
Every 3 years: Check tax-saving scope and ELSS lock-in status.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio mix. If any category (like small cap) has grown to more than 10% above your target, shift some money to underweight categories. This keeps your risk in line with your plan.
plan.rationale: You’re 33, aggressive, and want to grow ₹25L to ₹5Cr in 10 years. That needs high growth. We put 35% in small caps and 25% in mid caps for speed. 15% in flexi cap for balance. 10% in ELSS saves tax. Your ₹25L is in an equity-like hybrid fund — safe but still growing. This mix aims for ~15% returns, which can get you to ₹5Cr if you stay invested.
risks: Stock markets can drop 30-40% in a bad year — your portfolio value will go down temporarily. This is normal.
Small and mid-cap funds are volatile — they go up fast but can fall hard too.
If you stop SIPs during a fall, you miss the chance to buy more at lower prices.
ELSS has a 3-year lock-in: you can’t withdraw that money early.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: Rebalance as you near 5Cr target and apartment purchase

aps.reasoning: Equity sleeve is 100% active: Small-cap, Mid-cap, Flexi-cap and ELSS funds are all actively managed with stock-picking mandates; none are index funds/ETFs. Rebalancing is only annual (passive-friendly) plus a 10% drift rule, so turnover is low. Costs are mentioned but not prioritised – expense ratios 1.0–1.2% are accepted because the funds have ‘strong returns’, so cost is secondary to past performance. Rationale for every fund cites active manager track record and category conviction, not broad-market exposure, indicating deep research emphasis. Horizon is explicitly 10–20 years with glide-path, fully long-term oriented. Portfolio activeness: all equity funds are active categories with no risk metrics pointing to index-like behaviour; hybrid slice is also an active fund. Hence portfolio is judged genuinely active.

pqs.reasoning:
goal_alignment — 0.8: Allocation is clearly built around the 5-Cr, 10-year target: 60 % in small- and mid-cap, 15 % flexi cap, step-up SIP, and a glide-path that starts de-risking only after year 10; all fits a 33-year-old aggressive investor. No separate short-duration bucket is earmarked for the 3-year luxury-flat goal, so partial deduction.
diversification — 0.7: Good spread across market-caps and four different AMCs; ELSS and hybrid add modest style diversification. Still 100 % India equity oriented; no international, gold, or duration-managed bond fund until the later glide years, so not full spectrum diversification.
risk_return_appropriateness — 0.7: Projected 14.9 % CAGR is aggressive but not fantasy for the chosen categories; investor labels himself aggressive and is comfortable with volatility. Nevertheless the plan concentrates 85 % in the most volatile segments of the market and does not model a downside path deeper than 10.9 %, so risk realism could be stronger.
internal_consistency — 0.9: Rationale, weights, SIP amounts, lumpsum parking, glide-path, risks and rebalancing rule all tell the same high-growth equity story; no conflicting messages. Minor nit: Equity Savings is labelled "Aggressive Hybrid" in the JSON but called "Equity Savings" in the text.
tax_efficiency — 0.9: Correctly identifies 30 % slab, uses ELSS for 80C, parks existing corpus in a fund that will be taxed as equity instead of slab, and explicitly states the tax advantage. Would need slightly more post-tax modelling to be perfect but is already well above average.
